1: Date: eleventh year, third month, first day — June 587 BCE, still before Jerusalem's fall. The cedar of Lebanon allegory responds to Pharaoh's implied claim to greatness comparable to Assyria's.
2: el mi damita vegadlecha ('whom are you like in your greatness?'): the rhetorical question that opens the comparison — Ezekiel will answer it with the Assyrian cedar, then with the Eden trees. The question's form presupposes that Pharaoh has compared himself to great powers; YHWH will use that comparison to bring him down.
3: hineh ashur erez balevanon ('consider: Assyria was a cedar in Lebanon'): erez (H730, cedar) — the royal tree of the ancient Near East, used for palace and temple construction throughout the region (Solomon's temple used Phoenician cedar; Sennacherib's annals boast of cutting cedars). The cedar in Lebanon as the paradigm of greatness: the tallest trees in the world, fragrant, prized, associated with divinity. vemareh yophi ('beautiful in appearance'): yapheh (H3303, beautiful/fair) — the same word used for the tree of the knowledge of good and evil in Genesis 3:6. Beauty that leads to pride and fall is a consistent biblical pattern.
4: mayim gidluhu ('the waters made it great'): tehom (H8415, the deep/primordial waters) raising the cedar — the cosmic deep waters that appear in Genesis 1:2 as the primordial chaos now serve a fertilizing function. The deep waters irrigating the cosmic tree connect this vision to the Eden imagery: the river in Eden (Genesis 2:10) watered the garden. The cedar fed by tehom is sustained by the same waters that the creation narrative assigns to YHWH's sovereignty.
5: al-ken gavhah qomato mikol atzei hassadeh ('therefore it grew taller than all the trees of the field'): the height as the result of the deep-water nourishment. The vertical axis — height — is the spatial metaphor for power and pride. What grows tallest is most visible, most admired, and most exposed to the wind. The anatomy of pride: nourished by resource; expressed in height; exposed in fall.
6: bise'ipotav qinenu kol-of hashamayim ('in its boughs all the birds of the sky nested'): the cosmic-tree motif — the great tree as the dwelling of all creatures. This imagery appears in Daniel 4:12 (Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the cosmic tree under which all beasts sheltered) and in Jesus's parable of the mustard seed (Matthew 13:32: 'birds of the air come and perch in its branches'). The tree that shelters all living things is both imperial symbol and creation-order image.
7: yapheh gadlo ve-orech daliyotav ('beautiful in its greatness, magnificent in the length of its branches'): yapheh again — beauty that will become the grounds of the comparison with Eden (Ezekiel 31:8-9) and therefore the grounds of the fall. What is most beautiful and most admired is closest to the pride that triggers judgment.
8: aratzim lo adaduhu began elohim ('the cedars in the garden of God could not match him'): the explicit Eden-garden comparison. gan elohim (H1588 + H430, 'garden of God') = the garden of Eden (cf. Ezekiel 28:13; Gen 2-3). The cedar has surpassed even the Eden trees — the ultimate cosmic comparison. This connects to the Eden-fall pattern of Ezekiel 28 (the prince of Tyre): the most beautiful creature in the divine garden is the one most prone to pride and fall.
9: yafe asitiv berob daliytav ('I made him beautiful with his many branches'): YHWH claims the beauty as his own gift — the creator made the cedar beautiful, which means the cedar's beauty belongs to the creator, not to the cedar. vayekanehu kol-atzei eden asher began haelohim ('so that all the trees of Eden in the garden of God envied him'): the Eden-trees envious of the supremely beautiful cedar. The divine garden as the standard of cosmic beauty, now surpassed by Assyria (= Egypt). The claim to be greater than Eden is the ultimate hubris.
10: tachath asher gavah qomato ('because it grew tall'): the exact mechanism of the fall — the height itself is the problem. vayyarem libbov bego'vo ('its heart was proud of its height'): rum lev (H7311 + H3820, heart lifted up/proud) — the proud heart that Deuteronomy 8:14 and 17:20 warned against. The tree's pride is described as an interior condition (a 'heart') — Ezekiel consistently anatomizes pride as an inward movement before it manifests in outward action.
11: vaettnenu beyad el-goyim aasor aaseh lo ('I gave him over to the hand of the mightiest of nations'): el-goyim ('the mightiest of nations') = Babylon, YHWH's designated executor. The Niphal aasor — 'to deal with/act against' — the conquering nation as the agent of the divine judgment against the proud tree. berish'ato gerashtihu ('I drove him out for his wickedness'): garash (H1644, drive out/expel) — the same word for Adam and Eve's expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3:24), and for the Canaanites' expulsion before Israel (Exodus 23:28-31). The pattern: beauty, pride, expulsion from the garden/realm.
12: vayikretahu zarim araritzei goyim ('foreigners — the most ruthless of nations — cut him down'): the great cedar felled by the most ruthless peoples. The branches on the mountains, ravines, valleys — the scattered branches of a fallen empire. yazavu tzilo kol-ammei aretz ('all the peoples of the earth departed from his shade'): the shelter that the tree provided (Ezekiel 31:6) is gone; the nations that dwelt in its shade now abandon it. Political alliances evaporate with power.
13: After the fall, the same birds and beasts that nested in the cedar (Ezekiel 31:6) now inhabit the fallen trunk — not as sheltered guests but as scavengers on the corpse. The fallen empire's infrastructure is occupied by its former dependencies. The reversal of the living tree's hospitality into the dead tree's exploitation by creatures is a powerful image of imperial collapse.
14: lema'an asher lo yigbehu kol-atzei hamayim ('so that no tree beside the waters may exalt itself'): the fall of the great cedar is a lesson for all great trees — no water-nourished power should lift itself in pride. netenum el-mot el-eretz tachetit ('for all who drink water are given over to death, to the depths of the earth'): the universality of mortality even for the greatest: mot (death) and eretz tachetit (the land beneath/Sheol) await all who grow tall. The phrase betouch benei adam el-yordei bor ('among the children of men, with those who go down to the Pit') — all human greatness shares the mortality that the Pit/Sheol represents.
15: beyom ridto sheolah ('on the day he went down to Sheol'): Sheol (H7585, the underworld/realm of the dead) — Ezekiel's abundant use of Sheol vocabulary in these Egypt chapters (Ezekiel 31:14-17; Ezekiel 32:17-32) is distinctive. kissiyt alav et-tehom ('I covered the deep over him'): the same tehom that nourished the cedar (Ezekiel 31:4) now becomes its burial shroud — the waters that supported its life become the waters of death. vatit'abbal alav levanon ('I made Lebanon mourn for him'): Lebanon personified, mourning the loss of its signature tree/empire — the landscape itself grieves.
16: miqqol mappelo hirgashti goyim ('I made the nations shake at the sound of his fall'): the resonance of a great empire's fall — nations trembling at the sound. vayinnachamu begei eretz tachetit kol atzei eden mivchar vetov levanon ('and all the trees of Eden... were comforted in the realm below'): the remarkable 'comfort' of the Eden-trees in Sheol at seeing the great cedar join them. The underworld receives the most exalted as equals; death is the great leveler.
17: vegam-hem itav yaredu sheolah ('they too went down to Sheol with him'): the tree's zeroa (H2220, arm/strength — those allied with Assyria) share its fate. The empire takes its dependents with it into death. el-chalalei-cherev ('to those slain by the sword') — military death as the path to Sheol, contrasted with the honorable burial of those who die at peace.
18: el-mi damita kachah bichavod uvegodel batzei eden ('to whom are you like in glory and greatness among the trees of Eden?'): the rhetorical question of Ezekiel 31:2 is answered: Pharaoh is like all the great trees of Eden, all of whom have gone down. vetushav veshachavta bein arelim ('you too will be brought down... and lie among the uncircumcised'): arel (H6189, uncircumcised) — in the OT, to lie with the uncircumcised in Sheol is to share the shame of those outside the covenant (cf. Ezekiel 32:19-32). The honored burial of the circumcised versus the shameful mass grave of the uncircumcised: Pharaoh's final address places him outside the dignity of covenant membership in death.
